name: Ukrainian SSR (1949–1991)

colors.symbolism: the revolutionary struggle of the working class
in distinguishing the Ukrainian SSR; sometimes interpreted as Ukraine's natural beauty (skies and rivers)

description: Two horizontal stripes — red over light blue — in a 2:1 ratio. In the upper hoist, a gold hammer crossed by a sickle surmounted by a gold-bordered red five-pointed star, all on the red field.
history: Adopted by decree of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Ukrainian SSR on 21 November 1949, replacing earlier Ukrainian SSR flags that had used a plain red field with Cyrillic abbreviations of the republic's name. The new flag fitted into the broader 1947–1953 standardisation of Soviet republic flags: a red ground with the hammer, sickle and star (matching the all-Union flag) plus a coloured stripe distinguishing each republic — light blue in Ukraine's case. The flag was used until 24 August 1991, when Ukraine declared independence; it was formally replaced by the present blue-and-yellow Ukrainian flag on 28 January 1992.

features.symbolism: red for the workers' revolution and the international communist movement; the light-blue lower stripe distinguishes the Ukrainian SSR from the all-Union flag
the alliance of industrial workers (hammer) and peasantry (sickle) under Soviet socialism
the ultimate triumph of communism on the five inhabited continents
